lesson_title: Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.back: A structure that tracks the sequence of function calls that led to the current point of execution, showing which function called which.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.text: Each function call adds a {{c1::stack frame}} to the call stack, and when the function returns, its frame is {{c2::removed}}.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.question: What would the call stack look like when paused at the comment?
cards.explanation: The call stack shows the chain: main called a, a called b, b called c.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.front: What information does a stack frame contain?
cards.back: Function name, line number where it was called, function parameters, and local variables.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.text: The call stack window shows the {{c1::current}} function at the top and {{c2::main()}} (usually) at the bottom.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.front: How can you view variables from a previous function in the call stack?
cards.back: Click on that function's stack frame in the call stack window. The debugger will show you that function's local variables and source code location.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.concept: Why the call stack is useful for debugging
cards.explanation:
The call stack helps you:
  • Understand how you got to current code (execution path)
  • See which function called the buggy function
  • Examine parameters passed from calling functions
  • Trace back through function calls to find root cause
  • Understand program flow in complex code
examples: // Crash in process() - check call stack
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.front: What does it mean if a function appears multiple times in the call stack?
cards.back: The function is recursive (calls itself) or is called multiple times in the chain. This is normal for recursive functions.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.text: The call stack grows {{c1::downward}} (or rightward) as functions are called, and {{c2::shrinks}} as they return.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.front: What causes a stack overflow error?
cards.back: Too many stack frames (usually from infinite recursion or very deep recursion), exceeding the stack's memory limit.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.question: What will happen with this code's call stack?
cards.answer: Stack overflow. The function calls itself infinitely, adding unlimited stack frames until the stack memory is exhausted.
cards.explanation: Each call adds a frame but never returns, causing the stack to grow without bound.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.explanation:
Using the call stack window:
  • Top frame = currently executing function
  • Click any frame to see its source location
  • View local variables for any frame
  • See parameters passed to each function
  • Trace execution path backward
  • Identify where bad values originated
examples: // Bug in calculate() called from process()
// Click process() frame to see what it passed
// Find it passed wrong parameter value
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.text: Each function call adds a {{c1::stack frame}}, and returns {{c2::remove}} it from the call stack.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack

cards.question: How would you use the call stack to debug this?
cards.answer: When it crashes, check the call stack to see: main() → process() → calculate(). Click on process() frame to see what value was passed to calculate().
cards.explanation: The call stack lets you trace back to see where bad values originated.
cards.source: 3.9 - Using an integrated debugger: The call stack
